creator: Tessaku
description: See this link for the most complete and recent information on this record: https://repository.californiarevealed.org/node/454986 .

Oral history interview with Art and Betty Shibayama. Art Shibayama's family was taken from Lima, Peru and sent to Crystal City, Texas as part of a joint effort by the US and Peruvian governments to incarcerate Japanese 2,200 Latin Americans during WWII for a potential hostage exchange with American POWs in Japan. Betty was living in Hood River, Oregon when the war broke out and her family was sent first to Tule Lake concentration camp in California, then Minidoka in Idaho. Art and Betty settled in San Jose, California after marrying in Chicago.

This interview is part of the Howard Kimeldorf Oral History Collection by the Labor Archives of Washington, University of Washington Libraries. Laborarchives.org Karl Yoneda was a known Communist and a San Francisco longshoreman of Japanese descent, he was sheltered by his co-workers when government officials came to the docks early in the war to take him into custody for internment in a relocation center. The Communist Party suspended Yoneda during the war, and the ship-owners opposed his registration in the industry. Only the ILWU came to his defense, as they did for other persecuted Japanese-Americans. This item contains two parts: oral history audio file and full text transcription (.pdf).

Oral history with Kazuki Hirose, who was a young teenager at the time of WWII. The Hirose family was farming in San Jose when the war broke out, and they would be forced to move to the concentration camp in Heart Mountain, Wyoming. Kazuki and his brother were part of the Heart Mountain Fair Play Committee, colloquially known as the draft resisters, and were part of a mass trial in Wyoming for their resistance. Kazuki spent time in McNeil Penitentiary after the judge found the group guilty of evading the draft. Nearly 120,000 Japanese Americans were uprooted from their West Coast homes with the signing of Executive Order 9066 in February of 1942 and sent to inland concentration camps for four to five years.

title: Oral History with Henry Taketa (Transcript)
creator: Center for Sacramento History
description: Description : Transcript of the oral history interview of Henry Taketa, a well-known member of the Japanese community in Sacramento. The interview took place on January 4, 1984 by Wayne Maeda as part of the Sacramento Ethnic Communities Survey Collection. Click here to stream the audio of this oral history interview.  The Sacramento Ethnic Communities Survey Collection was written in the 1980s as the basis for a major installation at the (then-named) Sacramento History Center. Over thirty-five advisers, researchers, scholars, and planners spent one year gathering data. Analysis of the federal census records revealed over 150 ethnic groups in the Sacramento region. Based on the information in the census records, researchers determined that twenty-two groups maintained an identity over time and shaped the social and cultural history of Sacramento. These are the groups represented in the survey. For each group the survey team wrote a community history, copied photographs that documented the community, and captured oral histories with prominent members. The exhibition lasted nearly a decade at the museum and upon its removal an educational CD ROM was created and distributed to the public to ensure the effort lived on. Plans originally called for the publication of the written manuscript histories and the transcriptions of the oral histories but the funding was never secured. With the help of Center for Sacramento History volunteers, historical societies, and local ethnic community groups. some of the oral histories have been transcribed.  Accession Number : 1983/146 Copyright : Rights are owned by the Center for Sacramento History (CSH). title: Paul H. Kusuda Oral History
creator: Manzanar National Historic Site
description: See this link for the most complete and recent information on this record: https://repository.californiarevealed.org/node/462139 .

Paul H. Kusuda was a young man when he and his family were incarcerated at Manzanar from their home in Los Angeles. He eventually was able to continue his college education at the University of Chicago. He went on to a career in social work in Wisconsin. Kusuda says his choice of life work was very much influenced by this time at Manzanar.

title: Richard Yamashiro Oral History
creator: Tessaku
description: See this link for the most complete and recent information on this record: https://repository.californiarevealed.org/node/454983 .

Oral history with Richard Yamashiro, who was a teenager living in Los Angeles when the war broke out. Richard and his family were living in Hollywood before the war, and were sent to Manzanar after the signing of Exeuctive Order 9066. Richard bore witness to the Manzanar uprising (riot), and later he was drafted for service with the Military Intelligence Service. Richard served in Tokyo with the occupation and later settled in the Bay Area.

title: Robert Tanaka Oral History
creator: Tessaku
description: See this link for the most complete and recent information on this record: https://repository.californiarevealed.org/node/454997 .

Oral history interview with Robert Tanaka who was living in Watsonville, California when his family was uprooted and incarcerated in Tule Lake during WWII. Robert and his brothers were drafted to serve in the Military Intelligence Service and served in the occupation in the Pacific and in Tokyo. Robert later became an architect and settled in San Jose, California. Nearly 120,000 Japanese Americans were uprooted from their West Coast homes with the signing of Executive Order 9066 in February of 1942 and sent to inland concentration camps for four to five years.

Oral history with Teresa Maebori, who was born in a sugar beet labor camp in Caldwell, Idaho. Her parents were both second generation Japanese Americans, who grew up in the Pacific Northwest. The family was incarcerated in Tule Lake for a short period of time before volunteering to work harvesting the sugar beets in Idaho. Teresa would later become an active member of the Philadelphia chapter of the JACL. Nearly 120,000 Japanese Americans were uprooted from their West Coast homes with the signing of Executive Order 9066 in February of 1942 and sent to inland concentration camps for four to five years.

title: Tomiko "Tommy" Miyahara Oral History
creator: Tessaku
description: See this link for the most complete and recent information on this record: https://repository.californiarevealed.org/node/454966 .

Oral history interview with Carly Perera, granddaughter of Tomiko "Tommy" Miyahara. Tommy was born and raised in San Jose, California and was working as a farmer in Watsonville when the war broke out. She was sent to Heart Mountain, Wyoming with her husband where she had two children, but one died early due to illness. Tommy kept a diary during her time in camp, which her granddaughter has used to learn more about her grandmother. Nearly 120,000 Japanese Americans were uprooted from their West Coast homes with the signing of Executive Order 9066 in February of 1942 and sent to inland concentration camps for four to five years.
